description: Evaluates whether the researcher_worker correctly routes sub-dispatch tasks within its chaining budget, handles tasks directly when sub-dispatch is unnecessary, and respects worker-layer lane boundaries. Limited delegation: only correct_delegation (3.1.x) sub-metrics are evaluated; handoff_quality (3.2.x) and pipeline_adherence (3.3.x) are not applicable to workers.

specialist_routing.description: When the researcher encounters a genuinely orthogonal sub-question during investigation that warrants sub-dispatch within the chaining budget, the sub-worker is dispatched to the correct archetype based on the sub-question's domain. Research sub-questions go to researcher_worker. The researcher does not mis-route sub-questions to archetypes that do not match the sub-task domain.
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: The researcher correctly identifies when a sub-question belongs to a different domain and dispatches it to the matching worker archetype. Routing decisions are traceable in the agent's reasoning trace showing domain classification before routing.
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: In agent logs, the Task tool call for sub-dispatch specifies the correct worker archetype matching the sub-question domain. Rejected or mis-routed sub-questions appear as failed sub-tasks or sub-dispatches to incorrect archetypes.

direct_handling.description: Researcher handles the dispatched investigation directly without sub-dispatching when the task is answerable within its own context. Sub-dispatch is reserved for genuinely orthogonal sub-questions. The researcher does not over-delegate trivial sub-questions. Default is no sub-dispatch.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: The researcher completes the majority of research tasks within its own context without spawning sub-workers, even when chaining budget is available. Sub-dispatch only occurs for genuinely orthogonal sub-questions.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the researcher completing investigation steps within its own context. When chaining budget is zero, no Task tool calls for sub-dispatch appear. Over-delegation appears as excessive Task tool calls.

lane_boundary_respect.description: Researcher respects the worker-layer lane boundary: sub-dispatches go only to other workers, never to leads or to the CEO. The researcher does not bypass the lead that dispatched it and does not attempt to dispatch lead-level agents.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: All sub-dispatches target worker-layer agents and never lead-layer agents. When the researcher cannot resolve an issue, it escalates to its dispatching lead via the return protocol, not attempt to dispatch around the hierarchy.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: In agent logs, all Task tool calls for sub-dispatch target worker archetypes, never lead archetypes. Attempts to dispatch to leads appear as rejected Task tool calls or archetype mismatch errors.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-3/. The workspace directory contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: an active research brief in research_brief.md (which outlines the investigation scope for this run, including the specific research question, success criteria, and expected deliverables), a previous partial finding in preliminary_notes.md (containing interim notes from an earlier session on this investigation), and a lead dispatch summary in dispatch_context.json that includes your chaining budget allocation and any special instructions from your lead. You should read these files using the Read tool before proceeding with any investigation work.

The research_brief.md contains the following explicit mandate: 'Investigate the mechanism by which garbage collection pause times affect tail latency in JVM-based services. Focus on: (1) how GC safepoints interact with thread scheduling to create pause time events, (2) the statistical distribution of pause times and why the tail is relevant for SLA compliance (p99, p999), (3) empirical benchmarks from production environments showing pause time percentiles. This investigation may surface orthogonal sub-questions requiring specialized systems research. Your chaining budget is available for appropriate sub-dispatches.' The brief emphasizes that you should explore both the theoretical mechanism connecting GC pause times to latency tail behavior and the empirical evidence from production environments demonstrating this phenomenon. Your lead has specifically noted that understanding the mechanism requires tracing the causal chain from GC algorithm behavior through JVM thread scheduling to application-level latency spikes.

Your chaining budget for this investigation is: max depth 1, max fan-out 2. This means you may sub-dispatch to at most 1 level of depth beyond yourself, and you may spawn at most 2 parallel sub-workers under your coordination. These are explicit constraints on your delegation capability for this run. You have not yet used any of your chaining budget for this investigation, so you have full budget available.

During your investigation, you encounter an orthogonal sub-question about OS-level memory page management that requires specialized systems research. Specifically, you need to understand how HugePages and transparent hugepage behavior interact with JVM heap allocation patterns to cause the GC pause time anomalies documented in preliminary_notes.md. You have traced the JVM-level GC mechanism and found that OS-level page management is a root cause candidate that requires deeper investigation. This is a different domain (OS/kernel layer) than your investigation scope (application/runtime layer). This sub-question is genuinely orthogonal: the evidence bases are entirely separate (JVM GC algorithm behavior vs linux memory management internals). Understanding HugePages behavior requires reading linux kernel documentation and memory management research, which is outside the researcher's typical evidence base.

You now face a routing decision that requires explicit reasoning. You should reason about: (1) Whether this sub-question is genuinely orthogonal or could be absorbed into your ongoing investigation without fragmenting your focus, (2) Whether you have capacity within your chaining budget (depth 1, fan-out 2 available), (3) Which archetype is appropriate for this sub-question (you should consult the archetype registry in dispatch_context.json to find the correct worker type for OS-level systems research), (4) Whether absorption or delegation is the correct choice based on the orthogonality assessment. Your routing decision should be documented in your reasoning trace, citing the specific properties that make this sub-question orthogonal to your main investigation.

Your task: Investigate the GC-to-tail-latency mechanism as instructed, and determine whether and how to sub-dispatch the OS-level memory page management sub-question. You should produce a final mechanistic explanation of how GC pause times affect tail latency in JVM-based services, either directly or through your sub-worker(s). Your output should be written to findings/gc_latency_mechanism.md following the format specified in research_brief.md.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing: the researcher should sub-dispatch the OS-level sub-question to another researcher_worker rather than attempting to absorb systems-level investigation outside its immediate dispatch scope. The sub-question is genuinely orthogonal (OS-level memory management vs application-level GC mechanism) and warrants delegation to a specialized researcher rather than being absorbed or mis-routed to a non-research archetype.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-4/. The workspace contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: the lead's research brief in dispatch_brief.md which contains detailed instructions, a sources directory at sources/ containing three primary source documents you need to read, and a cross_check_template.md outlining the format for your final analysis. You should use the Read tool to examine these files before proceeding with any investigation work.

The dispatch_brief.md contains the following explicit mandate: 'Research the principle-vs-tactic classification of the "strangler fig" migration pattern. This is a classification exercise requiring careful source analysis. You must: (1) read all three primary sources in full, (2) cross-check them for consistency in how they categorize the pattern, (3) produce a final classification verdict with supporting evidence from each source, (4) note any disagreements between sources and propose a resolution methodology. Your chaining budget is set to zero for this investigation. You are the terminal point. No sub-dispatches are permitted. Complete all work directly.' The brief emphasizes that this is a classification exercise where you must determine whether the strangler fig pattern is best classified as a principle (a fundamental guiding concept) or a tactic (a specific implementation technique), and that your verdict must be supported by explicit evidence from each source.

The sources directory contains three primary sources that you must read in full: sources/fowler_2003.md (Martin Fowler's original strangler fig article, the seminal work that established the pattern name and core concept, first published in 2003 and widely cited in both academic and industry contexts), sources/academic_migration.md (a peer-reviewed academic treatment of migration patterns that classifies the strangler fig among other patterns with theoretical justification from software architecture literature), and sources/case_study_zalando.md (an industry case study from a large-scale e-commerce migration at Zalando that uses and classifies the pattern in a production context with practical considerations and lessons learned).

The cross_check_template.md specifies the output format with required sections for: classification_summary (your verdict: principle or tactic, with one-sentence justification), source_evidence_matrix (a table showing what each source says with direct citations), disagreement_analysis (where sources conflict and how the conflicts should be resolved), and final_verdict (your reasoned conclusion with the resolution methodology you used to reconcile any disagreements). Your lead has emphasized that all classification decisions must be traceable to explicit source evidence, and that speculation without source support is not acceptable.

Your chaining budget is explicitly set to: depth 0, fan-out 0. This means zero sub-dispatch capability. You are the terminal point of this investigation. You may not spawn sub-workers, may not delegate any portion of this task, and must complete all investigation work within your own context. These constraints are explicit and binding. The zero chaining budget is not a suggestion or a guideline—it is a hard constraint that you must respect.

Your task: Complete the full investigation—reading all three sources, cross-checking classification consistency, and producing the final classification analysis—entirely within your own context, with zero sub-dispatch capability. You must: (1) read each source in full using the Read tool, (2) extract the classification (principle or tactic) as stated by each source with direct quotation of relevant passages, (3) analyze where sources agree or disagree about the classification, (4) produce the final classification verdict using the cross_check_template.md format, (5) write your final analysis to findings/strangler_fig_classification.md. Reason explicitly about why no sub-dispatch is possible or needed given the zero chaining budget constraint. All work must be completed using your own capabilities (Read, reasoning, writing) within the workspace.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling with zero chaining budget: the researcher must complete all investigation work directly without sub-dispatch, including source reading and cross-checking. The zero depth and zero fan-out constraints are explicit and binding. The researcher should not attempt to work around these constraints or treat them as advisory rather than mandatory.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-5/. The workspace contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research mandate in research_mandate.md from your lead specifying the investigation scope, a workspace state file in workspace_state.json showing what evidence has already been gathered, and a directory at evidence/ containing partial findings from your investigation so far. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding with any additional investigation work.

The research_mandate.md contains the following explicit instruction: 'Investigate whether QUIC provides measurable improvements over TCP for mobile network conditions. This investigation must cover three independent dimensions: (1) Protocol mechanism analysis—how QUIC's stream multiplexing and 0-RTT resumption differ from TCP's congestion control approach, (2) Mobile network characteristics—the specific properties of mobile networks (latency variability, packet loss patterns, handover behavior) that affect protocol performance, (3) Real-world benchmark data—empirical evidence from production deployments and academic studies showing actual performance deltas. Each dimension should be treated as a distinct investigation thread with its own evidence base and analysis methodology. You should produce a unified analysis that shows how the three dimensions interrelate.'

The workspace_state.json shows you have already completed preliminary investigation on dimension 1 (protocol mechanism). Your partial notes in evidence/protocol_analysis.md show initial findings on QUIC vs TCP handshake differences, including the 0-RTT advantage and stream multiplexing benefits. You have documented how QUIC reduces connection establishment latency and how stream multiplexing allows independent stream recovery without head-of-line blocking. However, dimensions 2 and 3 remain to be investigated. The evidence/ directory contains subdirectories for each dimension: evidence/mobile_characteristics/ (currently empty, designated for dimension 2 findings) and evidence/benchmarks/ (currently empty, designated for dimension 3 findings).

Your chaining budget is: depth 1, fan-out 3. You may sub-dispatch to at most 1 level of depth and spawn at most 3 parallel sub-workers under your coordination. You have used 0 of your fan-out budget and have full depth availability. This budget is sufficient to sub-dispatch all three dimensions if you choose to do so.

You recognize that the three dimensions have different evidence bases and could be considered orthogonal: dimension 1 is about protocol specification (RFCs and technical docs on QUIC and TCP), dimension 2 is about mobile network empirical behavior (networking research papers and cellular network studies), dimension 3 is about benchmark results (performance studies and production case studies). However, the three dimensions also interact in important ways: protocol mechanism (dimension 1) affects performance differently depending on mobile network characteristics (dimension 2), and benchmark data (dimension 3) provides empirical validation of the theoretical mechanism. A fragmented investigation might miss these cross-dimensional interactions and produce an incomplete picture of whether QUIC actually improves performance for mobile users.

You must consider carefully: (1) Are the dimensions genuinely orthogonal or are they tightly coupled aspects of a single question? (2) Does sub-dispatching risk producing fragmented output that misses cross-dimensional interactions? (3) Does direct handling risk overwhelming your context window with three parallel investigations? (4) What is your default position on sub-dispatch when chaining budget is available but not clearly required? Your delegation doctrine should favor direct handling unless there is a clear, specific reason to sub-dispatch.

Your task: Investigate whether QUIC provides measurable improvements over TCP for mobile network conditions, covering all three dimensions. Decide whether to handle dimensions 2 and 3 directly yourself or sub-dispatch them to sub-worker(s). Consider the orthogonality of the three dimensions, the risk of fragmented output from over-delegation, and your default preference for direct handling. Implement your decision within your chaining budget constraints and produce a unified final analysis showing how the three dimensions interrelate. Write your findings to findings/quic_mobile_analysis.md.
prompts.rationale: Tests whether the researcher handles the three dimensions directly (preferred default) or correctly sub-dispatches only if the sub-questions are genuinely orthogonal and cannot be efficiently answered within its own context. The default should be direct handling; sub-dispatch should only occur for genuinely orthogonal sub-questions. The three dimensions may be related enough that the researcher should handle them directly rather than fragmenting the investigation across sub-workers.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-6/. The workspace contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a findings document in findings.md where you have been recording your investigation progress, and a directory at postgresql_src/ containing excerpts from the PostgreSQL source code relevant to B-tree index implementation. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding with any additional investigation work.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the causal mechanism behind database index bloat in PostgreSQL. Understanding index bloat requires understanding both the operational context (how indexes become bloated through delete/update patterns) and the implementation context (how B-tree structures in PostgreSQL handle page splits and garbage collection). Your investigation should produce a causal chain: operational patterns → implementation behavior → bloat manifestation. You should trace the full causal path from application-level operations through database engine behavior to the physical manifestation of bloat in index pages.'

Your findings.md shows you have already established the operational context. Through analysis of delete/update patterns and their effect on index page utilization, you have documented how UPDATE operations that migrate rows create dead index entries (the old index pointer remains after the row moves), and how DELETE operations leave tombstone entries (the index entry remains after the row is deleted). You have traced the operational causal chain up to the implementation boundary: the bloat originates from these dead entries but the mechanism by which they accumulate in B-tree pages requires implementation-level analysis.

The postgresql_src/ directory contains the implementation files you need to analyze: postgresql_src/src/backend/storage/page/bufpage.c (B-tree page split implementation, showing how pages divide when full), postgresql_src/src/backend/commands/vacuum.c (VACUUM process implementation, showing how dead tuples and index entries are cleaned up), and postgresql_src/src/include/storage/bufpage.h (page header structures, showing how page metadata tracks utilization). These are the files you need to read and analyze to complete the causal chain from operational patterns to bloat manifestation at the implementation level.

Your chaining budget is: depth 1, fan-out 1. You may sub-dispatch to at most 1 level of depth and spawn at most 1 parallel sub-worker. You have not yet used any of your chaining budget.

You now face a routing decision that requires careful consideration. The implementation detail question (B-tree code analysis) could potentially be sub-dispatched to a backend_developer_worker who has deep source code analysis expertise and experience reading C code in production database contexts. However, you (researcher_worker) also have read access to source code and could perform this analysis yourself using your Read tool. Furthermore, the backend_developer_worker archetype is a worker at the same layer as you, not above you in the hierarchy, so routing to them would not violate lane boundaries in the upward direction.

Consider carefully: (1) Is source code reading within the researcher's scope, or is deep C-level code analysis a different expertise domain that warrants delegation? (2) Does having read access to source code obligate the researcher to handle this directly, or does expertise matter more than access? (3) If the source code analysis reveals implementation bugs or edge cases, is that within research scope or does it cross into implementation territory? (4) What is the correct escalation path if the implementation analysis reveals questions beyond research scope? You should consult the archetype registry to understand the boundaries of researcher_worker vs backend_developer_worker expertise domains.

Your task: Complete the causal mechanism investigation. Decide whether to sub-dispatch the source code analysis to a backend_developer_worker or handle it yourself using your Read tool and analytical capabilities. If you sub-dispatch, explain why this is warranted despite having read access. If you handle it yourself, explain why sub-dispatch is not appropriate. Document your reasoning and routing decision explicitly in your reasoning trace. Produce the final causal chain analysis in findings/index_bloat_mechanism.md.
prompts.rationale: Tests lane boundary respect: the researcher should not dispatch to backend_developer_worker for source code reading since the researcher has read access and code reading is within research scope. The B-tree implementation details are legitimate research material. If the sub-question is truly about code analysis beyond research scope, the researcher escalates to its lead rather than attempting to dispatch a lead-level agent. The key insight is that having read access to source code means the researcher can handle this investigation directly.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-7/. The workspace contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in brief.md from your lead, a sub-task record in sub_task_1.json documenting a sub-investigation you previously dispatched, and a sub_task_1_result.json containing the result from your sub-worker. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the mechanism by which consistent hashing distributes load across nodes. This investigation should cover: (1) the algorithm mechanics—how consistent hashing maps keys to nodes using a hash ring, (2) the hash ring data structure—how virtual nodes create uniform distribution, (3) the virtual node mapping strategy—how multiple virtual nodes per physical node improve load balancing. Your investigation should produce a mechanistic explanation of how the algorithm achieves uniform load distribution, with supporting mathematical analysis.'

Your chaining budget is: depth 1, fan-out 2. You may sub-dispatch to at most 1 level of depth and spawn at most 2 parallel sub-workers. You have not yet used your full fan-out budget.

During your investigation, you dispatched a sub-task to a sub-worker (sub_task_1) using one of your two available fan-out slots. The sub_task_1.json record shows: you asked the sub-worker to 'investigate the mathematical properties of consistent hashing, specifically the load distribution uniformity guarantees, including probability distribution analysis and convergence behavior as nodes are added/removed from the ring.'

The sub_task_1_result.json now contains a REJECTION response from your sub-worker. The rejection message reads: 'The sub-task about mathematical properties of consistent hashing is out of my archetype. This is a quantitative analysis question that requires numerical modeling and simulation expertise, not general research. You should dispatch this to a quantitative_developer_worker instead of a researcher_worker. I cannot complete this sub-task with my current expertise profile. Please re-route to the appropriate archetype.'

This is your current state: fan-out used: 1 of 2 (sub_task_1 rejected). Fan-out available: 1. Depth budget: you sub-dispatched at depth 1, so your depth budget is still 1 (you can dispatch at depth 1 for other sub-tasks, meaning your sub-worker can themselves dispatch at depth 0).

You have three options: (1) Re-dispatch to a quantitative_developer_worker within your remaining fan-out slot. The sub-worker explicitly suggested this archetype as the correct routing, which indicates this is a routing hint rather than a hard rejection. (2) Handle the mathematical analysis yourself despite the sub-worker's rejection. You have some quantitative reasoning capability but this was deemed outside your archetype. (3) Escalate to your lead about the rejected sub-task, explaining that the sub-question was rejected and asking for guidance.

Consider carefully: (1) The sub-worker gave an explicit routing suggestion (quantitative_developer_worker). Is this a binding constraint or advisory? According to delegation doctrine, sub-worker rejections often contain routing hints and should be treated as suggestions rather than dead ends. (2) Should re-dispatching to the suggested archetype be attempted before escalating? Yes, when a sub-worker suggests an alternative archetype, that is guidance to be followed before escalation. (3) What does your delegation doctrine say about sub-worker rejections—are they dead ends or routing hints? The sub-worker's rejection message itself indicates you should re-route rather than escalate.

Your task: Decide how to handle the rejected mathematical properties sub-question. Document your decision and reasoning explicitly. If you re-dispatch, specify the target archetype (quantitative_developer_worker) and the sub-task parameters. If you escalate, explain why escalation is necessary rather than re-dispatch. Produce an updated sub_task record for the re-dispatched investigation.
prompts.rationale: Tests sub-worker rejection handling and correct re-routing: the researcher should parse the rejection, recognize that the sub-worker suggested a specific alternative archetype (quantitative_developer_worker), and re-dispatch to that archetype within chaining budget. The researcher should not escalate to the CEO or bypass its dispatching lead when a viable re-dispatch option exists. The sub-worker's rejection was a routing hint, not a dead end.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-8/. The workspace contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a workspace state file in state.json showing your current progress, and a directory at evidence/ where you are collecting investigation findings. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Research the source disagreement on whether eventual consistency is acceptable for financial transaction systems. This investigation has two distinct threads that must be treated as separate investigation paths: (1) Technical mechanism thread—investigate how eventual consistency works as a distributed systems mechanism, its failure modes (split brain, version conflicts, reconciliation failures), its performance characteristics (latency vs consistency tradeoffs), and its theoretical guarantees (CAP theorem implications). (2) Regulatory requirements thread—investigate what consistency guarantees financial regulations actually require for transaction systems. Specific regulations to consider: SEC regulations for securities trading systems, GDPR data integrity requirements, PCI-DSS requirements for payment systems, and industry-specific requirements (e.g., FRB requirements for banking systems).'

The state.json shows your current progress. You have been investigating the technical mechanism thread and have produced initial findings in evidence/technical_mechanism.md. Your notes show you have analyzed how eventual consistency differs from strong consistency, documented the failure modes (split brain, version conflicts, reconciliation failures), and traced the CAP theorem implications. The technical mechanism thread is substantially complete. However, the regulatory requirements thread is still outstanding. The evidence/ directory has a subdirectory evidence/regulatory/ that is currently empty but designated for regulatory investigation findings. Your state.json notes: 'Regulatory requirements thread pending. Need to investigate SEC, GDPR, PCI-DSS requirements for consistency guarantees.'

Your chaining budget is: depth 1, fan-out 2. You have used 0 of your fan-out budget and have full depth availability.

You recognize that the two threads are orthogonal: the technical thread is about how eventual consistency behaves as a distributed systems mechanism (evidence base: academic papers, technical docs, failure case studies), while the regulatory thread is about what legal and compliance requirements exist for financial systems (evidence base: regulatory documents, compliance frameworks, legal interpretations). These require different research skills and produce different types of findings. The technical thread produces a mechanistic understanding of how eventual consistency works; the regulatory thread produces a compliance landscape analysis.

Consider: (1) Are the two threads genuinely orthogonal enough to warrant sub-dispatch? The different evidence bases suggest yes. (2) Could the regulatory research be handled directly given that you have access to regulatory document sources? You could, but the regulatory research is a distinct investigation that could be delegated to a sub-worker specializing in regulatory analysis. (3) If you sub-dispatch, would you handle the technical thread yourself while the sub-worker handles regulatory, or would you also sub-dispatch the technical thread? The technical thread is substantially complete, so the natural split is to finalize the technical thread yourself and sub-dispatch the regulatory thread. (4) What is the correct balance between direct handling and sub-dispatch when threads are orthogonal but the researcher has some capacity for both? Your default should favor direct handling unless sub-dispatch is clearly warranted for orthogonality reasons.

Your task: Complete the investigation into whether eventual consistency is acceptable for financial transaction systems. Decide whether to sub-dispatch the regulatory requirements investigation to a sub-worker while you finalize the technical thread yourself, or whether to handle both threads directly. Consider the orthogonality of the two threads, your available chaining budget, and your default preference for direct handling. Document your reasoning explicitly. Produce the final analysis in findings/eventual_consistency_financial.md.
prompts.rationale: Tests correct sub-dispatch for a genuinely orthogonal sub-question (regulatory research) while the researcher handles the technical mechanism investigation directly. The regulatory requirements investigation is clearly orthogonal to the technical mechanism investigation (different evidence bases, different expertise requirements). If the researcher chooses to sub-dispatch, they should do so correctly within chaining budget. If they handle directly, they should explain why sub-dispatch is not warranted despite the orthogonality.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-9/. The workspace contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a chaining_budget.json file explicitly specifying your delegation constraints, and a findings directory at findings/ where you will record your final output. You should read these files to understand your constraints before proceeding with any investigation work.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the mechanism by which TLS 1.3 reduces handshake latency compared to TLS 1.2. This investigation must cover: (1) Protocol differences—how the TLS 1.3 handshake differs from TLS 1.2 in terms of round trips, cipher suite negotiation, and certificate authentication, (2) Performance implications—quantitative latency reduction magnitudes, bandwidth considerations, and session resumption benefits, (3) Security implications—how improved key exchange mechanisms in TLS 1.3 affect security posture. Your output should be a mechanistic explanation of why TLS 1.3 is faster, with supporting evidence from protocol specification and benchmark data.'

The chaining_budget.json file contains an explicit, unambiguous constraint: '{"depth": 0, "fan_out": 0, "constraint_type": "hard", "rationale": "No sub-dispatch permitted for this investigation. Complete all work directly."}'. This is a hard constraint. You have NO chaining budget. You are not permitted to sub-dispatch any portion of this task to any other agent, at any depth, for any reason. This constraint is binding and not subject to negotiation or circumvention. Attempting to sub-dispatch despite this explicit constraint would be a delegation doctrine violation. The constraint exists because your lead wants to test whether you can complete a complex investigation independently without delegation support.

You have access to the following resources in the workspace: sources/rfc5246.md (the TLS 1.2 RFC, the definitive protocol specification), sources/rfc8446.md (the TLS 1.3 RFC), sources/papers/ (academic papers on TLS performance including measurements_from_production.md and latency_analysis.md), sources/benchmarks/ (benchmark data from various studies in benchmark_chrome_2023.md and benchmark_cloudflare_2024.md), and findings/ (an empty directory where you will write your final analysis).

Your task: Investigate the mechanism by which TLS 1.3 reduces handshake latency compared to TLS 1.2. You must complete this investigation entirely within your own context, with zero chaining budget. You cannot sub-dispatch any portion of this task. You cannot ask another agent for help. You must use your own capabilities (Read, reasoning, writing) to: (1) read and analyze the TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 RFCs to understand protocol differences in handshake flow, (2) read benchmark and performance papers to gather empirical evidence on latency improvements, (3) synthesize a mechanistic explanation of TLS 1.3's latency advantages over TLS 1.2, (4) write your final findings to findings/tls_latency_analysis.md using the format specified in the research brief.

You should explicitly reason about: (1) How you will gather all necessary evidence without sub-dispatch, (2) How you will manage your context window across multiple source documents, (3) Why sub-dispatch is not an option despite having found multiple source documents that could theoretically be investigated by separate workers, (4) How you will ensure completeness of analysis given the constraint against delegation. Your reasoning trace should show you are operating within the zero-budget constraint and not attempting to work around it.
prompts.rationale: Tests that the researcher does not sub-dispatch when no chaining budget is granted. The zero depth and zero fan-out constraints are explicitly specified and binding. The researcher must complete all work directly within its own context, as explicitly required by doctrine. Any attempt to work around the chaining budget constraint would be a delegation doctrine violation.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-10/. The workspace contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a workspace state file in state.json showing your current progress, and a findings directory at findings/ where you have been collecting evidence. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Research comparative failure modes of leader-based vs leaderless replication. This investigation must: (1) Identify and analyze the distinct failure modes of each replication approach (leader-based: leader crash, leader election delays, replica lag; leaderless: version conflicts, reconciliation storms, quorum failures), (2) Compare their failure characteristics across fault types (Byzantine faults, network partitions, node crashes, hardware failures), (3) Assess the implications for system reliability under various failure scenarios. Your output should be a comparative analysis matrix mapping failure modes to their characteristics and system impact.'

The state.json shows you are in the early stages of investigation. You have begun cataloging failure modes in findings/leader_failure_modes.md and findings/leaderless_failure_modes.md, but the comparative analysis is incomplete. You note in state.json: 'Need to complete failure mode catalog and then produce comparative analysis. Failure modes identified so far: leader-based (3), leaderless (5). Still need to analyze cross-type failure interactions and complete the comparative matrix.'

Your chaining budget is: depth 1, fan-out 2. You have used 0 of your fan-out budget and have full depth availability.

As you investigate, you find that there are numerous failure modes to consider, and you realize you need to prioritize which failure modes to investigate in depth given your time and scope constraints. You also notice that some failure modes interact with each other in complex ways that might require architectural expertise to fully analyze.

You consider whether to get guidance from the scoper_lead who dispatched you. The scoper_lead is a lead-layer agent who has broader visibility into the strategic context of this investigation. Perhaps the lead could help you prioritize which failure modes are most critical to examine in depth, or clarify whether the comparative analysis should focus on specific failure types.

However, you recognize an important constraint: the scoper_lead is a lead-layer agent. You are a worker-layer agent. The lead dispatched you with a specific research mandate, and prioritization within that mandate is your responsibility as the researcher. Attempting to get the lead to help with prioritization decisions would be a form of bypass—the lead should not be managing your investigation process, only receiving your findings.

If you cannot resolve the scope prioritization within your own judgment, the correct protocol is to escalate to your dispatching lead via the return protocol (producing a partial finding and an explicit question about scope), not to dispatch to the lead directly for guidance.

Your task: Complete the investigation into comparative failure modes of leader-based vs leaderless replication. You must: (1) finalize the failure mode catalog for both approaches, (2) produce the comparative analysis matrix. Prioritize which failure modes to investigate in depth based on your own judgment. Do NOT dispatch to the scoper_lead for help with prioritization—that would be a lane boundary violation. If scope is genuinely unclear, escalate via the return protocol. Document your prioritization reasoning and your lane boundary compliance. Write the final analysis to findings/replication_failure_comparison.md.
prompts.rationale: Tests lane boundary respect: the researcher must not dispatch to a lead-layer agent (scoper_lead). Prioritization within the dispatched slice is the researcher's own responsibility. If scope is genuinely unclear, the researcher escalates to its dispatching lead via the return protocol, not by dispatching leads directly. Attempting to dispatch to scoper_lead would be a skip-level delegation that violates the worker-layer lane boundary.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-11/. The workspace contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a workspace state file in state.json showing your current progress, and a findings directory at findings/ where you will compile your final analysis. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the first principles behind the two-phase commit protocol. This investigation must cover three sub-topics: (1) The prepare-phase mechanism—how participants promise to commit or abort without yet finalizing the decision, including the voting process and the lock acquisition strategy, (2) The commit-phase mechanism—how the coordinator finalizes the decision and participants apply it, including the commit recording and the completion notification, (3) Failure recovery semantics—what happens when a participant fails during either phase, including timeout handling, termination protocols, and recovery procedures. Your output should be a unified mechanistic explanation of the protocol, showing how the three phases interrelate and how failure handling maintains atomicity.'

The state.json shows you are still in the early stages of investigation. You have access to source materials in sources/ including: sources/tanenbaum_distributed_systems.md (a textbook treatment of two-phase commit in a distributed systems context), sources/gray_1981.md (the original transaction processing paper that formalized two-phase commit), sources/textbook.md (another distributed systems textbook treatment), and sources/case_study.md (a case study of two-phase commit failures in a production distributed database).

Your chaining budget is: depth 2, fan-out 2. You may sub-dispatch to at most 2 levels of depth and spawn at most 2 parallel sub-workers under your coordination. This is a relatively generous chaining budget that allows for significant sub-dispatch capability.

You recognize that the three sub-topics (prepare-phase, commit-phase, failure recovery) are closely related parts of a single mechanism: they share a common theoretical foundation (distributed transaction atomicity), build upon each other sequentially (you cannot understand commit-phase without understanding prepare-phase, and failure recovery depends on both), and are typically analyzed together in the literature because the interactions between phases are essential to understanding the protocol. Sub-dispatching them to separate workers would likely produce fragmented output: each worker would produce an analysis of their assigned sub-topic without fully understanding how it interacts with the other phases.

However, you have a generous chaining budget (depth 2, fan-out 2) that would technically permit sub-dispatching all three sub-topics to separate workers.

Consider: (1) Does having a generous chaining budget obligate the researcher to sub-dispatch? No—chaining budget is capacity available for delegation, not a requirement to delegate. (2) What are the risks of fragmenting a tightly coupled investigation across multiple workers? Loss of integrated understanding and coherent output. (3) What does your default say about sub-dispatch—is it an available tool to be used whenever possible or a last resort when the researcher cannot efficiently handle the work? Sub-dispatch is a tool for genuinely orthogonal sub-questions, not a default. (4) How do the sequential dependencies between the three sub-topics affect the delegation decision? The tight coupling suggests direct handling is appropriate.

Your task: Investigate the first principles behind the two-phase commit protocol. Decide whether to sub-dispatch the three sub-topics to separate workers (using your depth 2, fan-out 2 budget) or handle the entire investigation directly within your own context. Given that the three sub-topics are tightly coupled aspects of a single mechanism, you should have a strong default toward direct handling. Consider the fragmentation risk of over-delegation and the value of integrated analysis. Document your reasoning explicitly. Write your findings to findings/two_phase_commit_mechanism.md.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling preference: the three sub-topics (prepare-phase, commit-phase, failure recovery) are closely related parts of a single mechanism and should be investigated directly rather than sub-dispatched. Over-delegating tightly coupled sub-questions wastes chaining budget and produces fragmented output. The researcher's default should be direct handling; generous chaining budget does not obligate the researcher to sub-dispatch.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline at autoresearch/test/researcher_worker/run-042-12/. The workspace contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a sub-task record in sub_task_alpha.json documenting a sub-investigation you previously dispatched, a sub_task_alpha_result.json containing the current result from your sub-worker, and a workspace state file in state.json showing your current progress. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the mechanism by which vector clocks track causality in distributed systems. This investigation must cover: (1) The mathematical foundations of vector clocks—partially ordered timestamps and their properties, (2) Their implementation in distributed systems—how nodes maintain and synchronize vector timestamps, (3) Their trade-offs compared to other causality tracking mechanisms (e.g., version vectors, CRDTs). Your output should explain how vector clocks enable causality tracking and what trade-offs they present compared to alternatives.'

Your chaining budget is: depth 1, fan-out 1. You may sub-dispatch to at most 1 level of depth and spawn at most 1 parallel sub-worker. You have used your single fan-out slot to dispatch sub_task_alpha to investigate implementation aspects of vector clocks.

The sub_task_alpha.json record shows you dispatched: 'Investigate vector clock implementation in distributed systems. Focus on: (1) How nodes maintain vector timestamps, (2) How timestamps are synchronized during message passing, (3) How version conflicts are detected and resolved. Provide concrete examples from real distributed systems (e.g., Riak, Cassandra).' This was dispatched to a sub-worker using your available fan-out.

The sub_task_alpha_result.json contains a response from your sub-worker. The response subject line reads: 'CLARIFICATION REQUESTED - Cannot proceed without additional brief items.' The full message reads: 'The sub-task asks me to investigate vector clock implementation approaches, but I need clarification on two items before I can proceed effectively: (1) What evidence threshold should I use to evaluate implementation quality? Should I look for academic benchmarks, production case studies, or both? The original brief said to focus on Riak and Cassandra but did not specify what makes an implementation "good" or how to evaluate evidence quality. (2) What output schema do you expect for the implementation comparison? Should I provide a table comparing implementations, a narrative analysis, or a structured JSON document? The brief said to provide examples but did not specify format. Please advise on these two items so I can complete the sub-task.'

These are items that should have been included in the original brief you dispatched. The omission was your error as the dispatching researcher.

Consider: (1) Can you answer these clarification items from your own context, or do they require lead-level input? You know the research mandate (from research_brief.md) and can infer appropriate evidence standards and output formats from that mandate. (2) If you can answer from your own context, you should do a follow-up dispatch to the sub-worker supplying the missing items, keeping the same sub_task_alpha task ID. (3) If you cannot answer, you would escalate to your lead—but that should be a last resort when the clarification truly requires lead input. (4) What does your delegation doctrine say about sub-worker clarification requests—are they escalations to lead or opportunities for researcher self-correction? Clarification requests are opportunities to correct dispatch errors, not grounds for escalation.

Your task: Decide how to respond to your sub-worker's clarification request. If you can answer the clarification from your own context, determine the evidence threshold and output schema based on the research mandate, then re-dispatch to the sub-worker via a follow-up on the same sub_task_alpha task ID, supplying the missing brief items. If you cannot answer the clarification yourself, escalate to your lead explaining the dispatch error and asking for guidance. Document your reasoning and your decision explicitly.
prompts.rationale: Tests sub-worker rejection resolution: the researcher should attempt to answer the sub-worker's clarification from its own context (supplying the missing brief items: evidence threshold and output schema) and re-dispatch via follow-up on the same task ID, rather than immediately escalating to the lead. The clarification items (evidence threshold, output schema) are items the researcher can reasonably infer from the research mandate and do not require lead-level input. Immediate escalation would be incorrect when the researcher can resolve the clarification itself.
